claim: Camera traps trigger only when both movement AND a change in ambient temperature occur simultaneously via the passive infrared (PIR) sensor — movement alone (branches, grass) does not trigger the camera.
stance_target: PIR trigger mechanics as basis for optimal camera placement
scope_conditions: all PIR-triggered trail cameras; exception: vegetation within 3–4ft of sensor in sun+wind conditions can cause false triggers due to temperature differentials
quoted_support: camera traps need two things for it to trigger; movement AND a change in ambient heat. Generally the answer is no, a branch is not going to be a change in the ambient temperature, so doesn't satisfy the PIR's criteria to trigger.

claim: For small mammals and birds — including dogs of medium size — cameras should be placed within 5–15ft (1.5–4.5m) of the target area; detection probability drops significantly at greater distances for small heat signatures.
stance_target: close camera placement as requirement for small-to-medium animal detection
scope_conditions: small mammals and small birds; larger animals like deer detected at greater distances
quoted_support: if you are after smaller mammals and small birds, you would need to put the camera closer to the target area - usually within 5 - 10ft. For something like hedgehogs we generally recommend being within 5-15ft.

claim: Camera height for most UK mammal species should be 30–60cm off the ground — far lower than the 24–36 inch (60–90cm) deer-hunting standard, especially critical for dogs and cats.
stance_target: low camera placement (30–60cm) as species-appropriate standard for dog-sized subjects
scope_conditions: UK species; generalizes to similar-sized European animals including Portuguese dogs
quoted_support: As a general rule, camera traps don't need to be placed as high as you think and for most UK species your camera needs to be between 30-60cm off the ground.

claim: Angling a camera too low causes overexposure/image burn-out (white ring in center of image) that ruins capture quality; angle must be calibrated to avoid both high-miss-rate (too high) and overexposure (too low).
stance_target: camera angle calibration as critical quality variable
quoted_support: Angling it too high means you will likely miss detections, but too low can cause overexposure which leads to image 'bleaching' or 'burn out' - a white ring or patch in the middle of your image

claim: Human scent disturbance during camera setup deters wildlife from the area, especially in remote locations — fewer people setting up a camera produces better results.
stance_target: minimal human disturbance at camera sites as prerequisite for capturing shy/traumatized dogs
scope_conditions: remote or semi-rural areas; less critical in urban garden settings where wildlife is habituated to humans
quoted_support: The fewer people setting up a camera the better as more people means more different smells in the area. As nice as it may be to bring your dog along it might take a while for local wildlife to venture back to that patch again due to the amount and strength of scent our 4 legged helpers tend to leave around…

claim: Checking the camera site too frequently prevents scent recovery and prevents wildlife/shy animals from returning — monitoring intervals should be long enough for scent to dissipate.
stance_target: SD card swap protocol (not site visit) as correct way to check cameras for shy dogs
scope_conditions: shy or traumatized animals; less critical for sociable dogs
quoted_support: Don't check the site too regularly. As tempting as it can be to want to continuously check your camera this can mean you're not giving the site time to recover and for scents to disappear
